w:r.w:t: Java Full Stack Development with React.js
Detailed Course Outline with Lab Manuals
120 Hours | 30 Sessions × 4 Hours | Instructor-Led | Freshers → Production-Ready Software Engineers
Core Java, OOP, Collections, Git, JUnit 5
Hibernate/JPA, Spring Boot, REST APIs, JWT Security
JavaScript ES6+, React.js, Redux Toolkit, Tailwind CSS
Docker, Jenkins CI/CD, Cloud Deployment, Capstone
PHASE 1 — FOUNDATIONS: Core Java & Developer Tooling
Introduction to Java, JVM, JDK, JRE
Install JDK 21 and verify with java -version and javac -version
Write your first Java program: HelloWorld.java with public static void main
Compile and run via command line
Explore JVM options: -Xmx, -Xms; observe process with jps
Declare variables of all 8 primitive types (byte, short, int, long, float, double, char, boolean)
Perform arithmetic, relational, and logical operations
Demonstrate integer overflow and type promotion
Implement FizzBuzz using if/else and switch-case
Print multiplication tables using nested for loops
Implement a number guessing game using while loop
Use break and continue with labeled loops
Create 1D and 2D arrays; use Arrays.sort() and Arrays.binarySearch()
Find the largest and second-largest element without sorting
Rotate an array to the right by k positions
Use varargs method to sum any number of integers
Write a Java program using all primitive types, loops, and conditionals. Push to GitHub.
Wrapper Classes, Type casting, String Handling
Create a BankAccount class with private fields, constructors, getters/setters
Add a copy constructor and toString() method
Demonstrate the this keyword in constructor chaining
Write a static factory method getInstance()
Use super() to invoke parent constructors
Demonstrate final class and final method restrictions
Define abstract Shape class with abstract double area()
Implement Circle, Rectangle, Triangle extending Shape
Create Drawable and Resizable interfaces with default methods
Show a class implementing multiple interfaces
Store different Shape subtypes in a Shape[] array; iterate and call area()
Benchmark String concatenation vs StringBuilder for 10,000 iterations
Build a mini OOP model (e.g., Shape hierarchy). Commit & push to Git.
Collections Framework: List, Set, Map, Queue
Catch specific exceptions: ArithmeticException, NumberFormatException, NullPointerException
Implement multi-catch and finally block with resource cleanup
Use try-with-resources with a custom AutoCloseable resource
Compare ArrayList vs LinkedList performance for add/remove operations
Use HashSet to remove duplicates from a list; use TreeSet for sorted order
Implement a phone book using HashMap; demonstrate getOrDefault and putIfAbsent
Simulate a ticket queue with ArrayDeque
Make a Student class implement Comparable by GPA
Use Collections.sort() to sort a list of Students
Write a Comparator to sort Students by name, then by GPA descending
Write a generic Pair<A,B> class and a generic Stack<T>
Build a utility method that prints any List<T>
Load app configuration from config.properties using Properties class
Demonstrate bounded type parameters with <T extends Number>
Implement a student grade manager using Collections. Handle exceptions gracefully. Push to Git.
— Git & GitHub — Version Control for Professional Developers
Local Repo: init, add, commit, log, diff
Remote Repos: clone, push, pull, fetch
GitHub: Pull Requests, Code Review workflow
Initialize a local Git repo with git init
Stage files with git add, commit with meaningful messages
Use git diff to inspect unstaged changes
Create a feature branch: git checkout -b feature/add-login
Make commits on both main and feature branch to simulate divergence
Merge the feature branch back to main
Use git rebase to linearize history
Push repo to GitHub; clone it in a different folder to simulate second developer
Pull changes from remote; simulate a merge conflict in the same file
Resolve the conflict manually and complete the merge
Use git stash to save work-in-progress before switching branches
Fork a peer's repository on GitHub
Create a feature branch, make changes, push to your fork
Open a Pull Request with a descriptive title and body
Review a peer's PR: leave inline comments, request changes, approve and merge
Collaborate: create a shared GitHub repo, raise a PR, review a peer's code, and merge it.
Write @Test methods for a Calculator class (add, subtract, multiply, divide)
Run tests in IntelliJ and observe green/red results
Use @BeforeEach to initialize a new object before every test
Use @AfterAll to close shared resources after all tests
Group related tests using @Nested inner classes
Use @ParameterizedTest with @ValueSource to test multiple inputs
Use @CsvSource to test methods with multiple parameters
Use @MethodSource with a static Stream to provide complex test data
Assert that invalid inputs throw the correct exception for each case
Mock a UserRepository dependency in a UserService test
Use when().thenReturn() to stub method responses
Verify that mocked methods were called with verify()
Write unit tests for your Collections exercise (Day-3). Achieve ≥ 80% coverage. Push tests to GitHub.
Core Java + Git + TDD — Mini code review session. Each participant presents their GitHub repo.
SQL Basics: SELECT, INSERT, UPDATE, DELETE
DISTINCT, Boolean operators: AND, OR, BETWEEN, LIKE, IN
Set Functions: COUNT, SUM, AVG, MIN, MAX
Install MySQL 8 and connect via MySQL Workbench
CREATE TABLE authors (id, name, country, birth_year)
INSERT 10 author records; SELECT all and verify
SELECT authors from a specific country using WHERE
Use LIKE '%Java%' to search for title substrings
Order results by price DESC and title ASC
COUNT total books per genre using GROUP BY
Find the average price of books by each publisher
Use HAVING to show only publishers with more than 5 books
Combine WHERE and HAVING in a single query
Insert a batch of books with a single INSERT statement
Update the price of all books in a specific category by 10%
Soft-delete a book using an is_active flag rather than DELETE
Use TRUNCATE vs DELETE and understand the difference
Create a MySQL database for a bookstore. Write SELECT queries with filters, grouping, and aggregations.
CROSS, INNER, LEFT, RIGHT, FULL OUTER JOINs
CREATE DATABASE / TABLE, ALTER TABLE, DROP TABLE
Primary Key, Foreign Key, Constraints, NULL handling
Indexes: what they are and why they matter
INNER JOIN books with authors to see books and their author names
LEFT JOIN to find all authors, including those with no published books
SELF JOIN employees table to find manager-employee pairs
Three-table JOIN: orders → books → customers in one query
ALTER TABLE to add a created_at TIMESTAMP column with DEFAULT NOW()
Add a FOREIGN KEY from orders.book_id to books.id
Add a UNIQUE constraint on customers.email
Use CHECK constraint to ensure price > 0
Run EXPLAIN SELECT on an un-indexed query; observe full table scan
CREATE INDEX on books.title; re-run EXPLAIN and compare
Create a composite index on (category, price)
Understand when NOT to add indexes (small tables, high-write columns)
Find customers who placed more orders than the average customer (correlated subquery)
List all books priced above the average price using a scalar subquery
Rewrite the above using a CTE (WITH clause) for readability
Use a recursive CTE to generate a hierarchy of categories
Add relationships to bookstore DB (Authors, Books, Orders). Write JOIN queries. Script everything in Git.
Window Functions: ROW_NUMBER, RANK, LEAD, LAG
Use ROW_NUMBER() to rank books by price within each category
Use RANK() and DENSE_RANK() and compare differences on tied values
Use LAG() to compare each order's total to the previous order
Use SUM() OVER (PARTITION BY) for running totals per customer
CREATE VIEW customer_order_summary as a query joining customers and orders
SELECT from the view just like a table
UPDATE an updatable simple view and verify the underlying table changes
DROP VIEW and understand when to use views vs CTEs
CREATE PROCEDURE place_order(customer_id, book_id, qty) to insert an order
Add transaction control: BEGIN, COMMIT, ROLLBACK inside the procedure
Use SAVEPOINT to partially rollback only part of a multi-step transaction
Call the procedure and verify atomicity by simulating a failure midway
Use EXPLAIN FORMAT=JSON on a complex JOIN query
Identify and resolve a missing index causing a full scan
Rewrite a slow correlated subquery using a JOIN for better performance
Use SHOW PROCESSLIST and KILL to manage slow queries
Write a stored procedure for bookstore order processing. Use transactions.
PL/SQL Block Structure: Declaration, Execution, Exception
Write a basic DECLARE/BEGIN/EXCEPTION/END block to print Hello from PL/SQL
Declare variables of different types (VARCHAR2, NUMBER, DATE, BOOLEAN)
Use %TYPE and %ROWTYPE to anchor variable types to table columns
Calculate a student's grade (A/B/C/F) using IF-ELSIF-ELSE
Print the first 10 Fibonacci numbers using a LOOP with EXIT WHEN
Iterate over a number range using FOR i IN 1..10 LOOP
Use an implicit cursor with SQL%ROWCOUNT to count affected rows in UPDATE
Declare an explicit cursor to fetch all books with price > 500
Use cursor FOR LOOP (simplest cursor iteration pattern)
Fetch one row at a time with FETCH…INTO and close cursor manually
Handle NO_DATA_FOUND and TOO_MANY_ROWS system exceptions
Create a user-defined exception for invalid_discount
Use SQLCODE and SQLERRM in the WHEN OTHERS handler
Write a procedure that logs errors to an error_log table
Write PL/SQL blocks to compute student grade summaries with error handling. Commit SQL scripts to Git.
SQL + PL/SQL Proficiency — SQL quiz + hands-on: JOIN queries, aggregations, and a stored procedure.
PHASE 3 — BACKEND: Hibernate, Spring Boot & REST APIs
Lab 10.1: Maven Project Setup with Hibernate
Create a Maven project; add hibernate-core and mysql-connector-java dependencies
Configure persistence.xml with MySQL connection details
Set hibernate.hbm2ddl.auto=create to let Hibernate generate schema
Verify Hibernate connects to MySQL and creates an empty schema
Create a Product entity class annotated with @Entity and @Table
Map fields: id (@Id, @GeneratedValue), name, price, category
Persist a Product using EntityManager.persist() and verify in DB
Annotate id with @NotNull, name with @Size(min=2, max=100), price with @Min(0)
Write a test that attempts to persist an invalid entity and catches ConstraintViolationException
Use a Validator to validate objects before persisting
Persist three products in one transaction; verify with a SELECT in Workbench
Find a product by ID using EntityManager.find()
Update a product by merging a detached entity
Remove a product and confirm deletion in the database
Map a 'Product' entity to a MySQL table using JPA. Persist and retrieve data.
Demonstrate the four lifecycle states: New, Managed, Detached, Removed
Use EntityManager.contains() and EntityManager.detach() to inspect state
Show that changes to a Managed entity are auto-flushed at transaction commit
Use EntityManager.refresh() to reload entity from DB
Create Order entity with a @ManyToOne to Customer
Add @OneToMany(mappedBy) on Customer.orders for the inverse side
Persist a Customer with 3 Orders in one transaction using CascadeType.PERSIST
Fetch orders for a customer and print them
Create Student and Course entities with a @ManyToMany relationship
Use a join table enrollment(student_id, course_id)
Enroll a student in two courses; unenroll by removing from the collection
Enable orphanRemoval=true and verify dependent entities are deleted
Write a JPQL SELECT q FROM Product p WHERE p.price > :minPrice
Use a named query @NamedQuery on the entity class
Write an aggregate JPQL query to find average price per category
Convert a JPQL query to a Criteria API query for type safety
Model an e-commerce schema (User, Product, Order, OrderItem) with JPA relationships. Run JPQL queries.
— Spring Core — DI, IoC & Spring MVC
Spring Beans: XML config vs annotations
Create a Spring project with spring-context dependency
Define a NotificationService interface with EmailNotification and SMSNotification implementations
Inject NotificationService into OrderService using @Autowired on constructor
Swap the implementation by changing @Primary annotation; no OrderService code changes needed
Create a Singleton bean and a Prototype bean; compare instance identities
Use @Scope("request") in a web context (requires Spring Web)
Register a BeanFactoryPostProcessor to modify bean definitions at startup
Create a LoggingAspect with @Before advice to log method entry
Add @AfterReturning to log the return value
Add @Around advice to measure method execution time in milliseconds
Create a Spring MVC project with spring-webmvc and a web.xml configuring DispatcherServlet
Write a @Controller with @GetMapping(/products) returning a model+view
Configure InternalResourceViewResolver to resolve JSP views
Submit a form with @PostMapping and redirect using "redirect:/products"
Create a simple Spring MVC app that handles a form submission. Wire beans using annotations.
Why Spring Boot? Convention over Configuration
Lab 13.1: Bootstrap a Spring Boot Project
Generate a project at start.spring.io with Web, JPA, MySQL starters
Review the generated pom.xml and @SpringBootApplication class
Run the app with mvn spring-boot:run; confirm Tomcat starts on port 8080
Change the port to 8081 in application.properties and restart
Add @Service and @Repository beans; verify they are discovered automatically
Use @ComponentScan to specify a custom base package
Create application-dev.properties and application-prod.properties with different DB URLs
Switch active profile with --spring.profiles.active=dev at runtime
Use @Value to inject properties into a bean
Use @ConfigurationProperties to bind a group of properties to a POJO
Change root log level to DEBUG in application.properties
Set a custom log level for your package only
Add a logback-spring.xml to configure rolling file appender
Use @Slf4j (Lombok) or LoggerFactory to add structured log messages
Bootstrap a Spring Boot project. Configure profiles for dev and prod. Run with embedded Tomcat.
— Building RESTful APIs with Spring Boot
REST principles: Resources, HTTP Verbs, Status Codes
Lab 14.1: Build a Product REST API
Create ProductController with @RestController and /api/v1/products base path
Return ResponseEntity<ProductDTO> with appropriate status codes (200, 201, 404)
Test all endpoints with Postman; verify correct HTTP status codes
Create ProductRequestDTO and ProductResponseDTO to separate API model from entity
Use @JsonProperty to rename a field in JSON output
Use @JsonIgnore to hide sensitive fields like internalCode
Configure Jackson globally to use snake_case naming strategy
Create a GlobalExceptionHandler class annotated with @ControllerAdvice
Handle ResourceNotFoundException with a 404 response and error body
Handle MethodArgumentNotValidException (validation errors) with 400 and field errors
Handle all other exceptions with a generic 500 handler; never expose stack traces to clients
Build a complete CRUD REST API for 'Products' with proper error handling and status codes. Test with Postman.
— Spring Data JPA — Repositories & Query Methods
Use productRepository.save(), findById(), findAll(), deleteById() in a service
Confirm Spring Data generates the implementation at runtime
Inspect the auto-generated SQL with spring.jpa.show-sql=true
Write findByCategory(String category) – Spring generates the SQL automatically
Write existsByName(String name) and countByCategory(String category)
Use Optional<Product> as return type for single-result finders
Add Pageable parameter to findAll(Pageable pageable) in service method
Return Page<ProductDTO> to include total pages and total elements in response
Implement keyset pagination for high-performance large datasets
Enable JPA auditing with @EnableJpaAuditing on the main class
Add @CreatedDate and @LastModifiedDate fields to a base entity class
Demonstrate @Transactional(readOnly=true) on service read methods for performance
Integrate Spring Data JPA with your REST API (Day-14). Add pagination and sorting to GET endpoints.
— Spring Data REST, Validation & API Documentation
Spring Data REST: auto-exposing repositories as REST
Annotate ProductRequestDTO with @NotBlank, @Min, @Size on relevant fields
Add @Valid in the controller method parameter to trigger validation
Return a structured error response with field name and message from @ControllerAdvice
Write a custom @PriceRange validator as a custom constraint annotation
Access http://localhost:8080/swagger-ui.html and explore auto-generated docs
Add @Operation and @Parameter annotations to improve endpoint descriptions
Add @Schema annotations on DTO classes to document fields
Expose all actuator endpoints in application.properties (management.endpoints.web.exposure.include=*)
Create a custom health indicator that checks database connectivity
Add spring-boot-starter-data-rest; observe auto-generated REST endpoints
Access the HAL browser to navigate hypermedia links
Hide a repository method from REST exposure using @RestResource(exported=false)
Add validation to your API. Generate Swagger UI documentation. Expose Actuator endpoints.
Why Security? Common vulnerabilities (OWASP Top 10 overview)
JWT (JSON Web Tokens): structure, signing, verification
Add spring-boot-starter-security; observe all endpoints now require Basic Auth
Create a UserDetailsService loading users from the database
Configure SecurityFilterChain with HttpSecurity to permit /auth/** and secure /api/**
Test secured endpoints with Postman using Authorization: Basic header
Add io.jsonwebtoken (jjwt) dependency for JWT support
Create JwtUtil class with generateToken(username) and validateToken(token) methods
Implement /api/auth/login endpoint that returns a JWT on valid credentials
Write a JwtRequestFilter extending OncePerRequestFilter to validate JWT on every request
Create ROLE_USER and ROLE_ADMIN in the users table
Enable @EnableMethodSecurity on the security config class
Test that USER role cannot delete; ADMIN can. Verify with different JWT tokens in Postman
Configure CORS in SecurityFilterChain to allow http://localhost:3000 (React dev server)
Register BCryptPasswordEncoder as a Spring bean
Encode passwords before storing in DB; verify in login that encoder.matches() works
Test full login flow: POST /auth/login → receive JWT → call protected endpoint with Bearer token
Secure your REST API with JWT. Implement login endpoint returning token. Protect routes by role.
Full Backend API — Participants demo their REST API: CRUD operations, validation, JWT security, Swagger docs.
PHASE 4 — FRONTEND: JavaScript, React.js & API Integration
JavaScript in the browser: DOM, events
Variables: var, let, const — scoping differences
Array methods: map, filter, reduce, find
Compare var hoisting issues vs block-scoped let and const with concrete examples
Write arrow functions and compare this binding with regular functions
Use template literals to build HTML strings with embedded expressions
Use destructuring on objects and arrays; use rest and spread with function arguments
Use .map() to transform an array of products into display strings
Use .filter() to show only products in stock
Use .reduce() to calculate the total cart value
Create a promise that resolves after 1 second using setTimeout
Rewrite the same logic using async/await; handle errors with try/catch
Use Promise.all() to run two fetch calls in parallel and wait for both
Parse the JSON response and render 10 post titles into a <ul> in the DOM
Add a click event listener to each item to show the post body in a modal
Use AbortController to cancel a slow fetch request
Build a JavaScript mini-app that fetches data from a public API and renders it in the DOM.
— Ajax, REST Consumption & Node.js / npm Basics
Ajax: XMLHttpRequest vs Fetch vs Axios
Node.js core modules: fs, path, http
Compare XMLHttpRequest, fetch(), and Axios for a GET request to JSONPlaceholder
Use Axios to POST a new resource; log the created object returned by the server
Add an Axios request interceptor to attach an Authorization header to every request
Handle Axios errors in a catch block; distinguish network errors from HTTP error status codes
Lab 19.2: Consuming Your Spring Boot API
Start the Spring Boot API from Day 17 (or use a running instance)
Use Axios to call GET /api/products from a plain HTML page
Handle the CORS preflight by verifying your Spring Boot CORS configuration
Implement a simple search form that calls GET /api/products?category=X on submit
Initialize a new npm project with npm init -y; review package.json
Install lodash and axios; observe node_modules and package-lock.json
Write a Node.js script using fs to read a JSON file and parse it
Add custom scripts in package.json: start, build, lint
Scaffold a Vanilla JS project: npm create vite@latest my-app -- --template vanilla
Run npm install and npm run dev; observe HMR (Hot Module Replacement)
Create two JS modules: utils.js and main.js using ES6 import/export
Build for production with npm run build and inspect the dist folder
Use Axios to call your Spring Boot API from a plain HTML/JS page. Understand CORS. Setup a Node project.
What is React? Virtual DOM, ecosystem
Rendering: conditional rendering, lists with map()
Lab 20.1: First React App with Vite
Scaffold React project: npm create vite@latest shop-app -- --template react
Delete boilerplate; create a clean App.jsx with a Hello React heading
Run npm run dev and open React DevTools in Chrome to inspect the component tree
Add a <header> with app name and a <main> content section
Create a ProductCard component accepting name, price, category as props
Use JSX expressions {} to render dynamic values and a ternary for in-stock badge
Create a ProductList component that accepts an array and maps over it to render ProductCards
Pass a products array as prop from App.jsx to ProductList
Add PropTypes validation to ProductCard: name (string, required), price (number, required)
Test what happens when a required prop is missing (console warning)
Use defaultProps to set fallback values for optional props
Destructure props directly in the function signature for cleaner code
Conditionally render a 'No products found' message when the list is empty
Use {condition && <Component />} syntax for showing/hiding a badge
Add a unique key={product.id} to each mapped item; remove it and observe the console warning
Implement a simple filter by category using array .filter() before .map()
Create a React app that displays a list of products fetched from your Spring Boot API. Use props to pass data.
useEffect: side effects, data fetching, cleanup
useRef: DOM refs and persisting values
Add a counter component with useState; increment, decrement, and reset buttons
Build a toggle to show/hide product details on click
Create a text search input with useState; filter product list in real-time
Understand that state updates are asynchronous; use functional update form: setCount(prev => prev + 1)
Fetch products from Spring Boot API inside useEffect with an empty dependency array []
Show a loading spinner while fetching; hide it when data arrives
Add error state to display a user-friendly message if fetch fails
Implement cleanup: cancel fetch on component unmount using AbortController
Move cart state from CartIcon up to App.jsx so multiple components can read it
Pass cart state and an addToCart function down to ProductCard and CartPage as props
Use useRef to focus the search input automatically when the page loads
Use useRef to store a previous value without triggering a re-render
Lab 21.4: Context API for Global State
Create CartContext with createContext(); provide it at the App level with CartContext.Provider
Consume cart state in CartIcon and CartPage using useContext(CartContext)
Add addToCart and removeFromCart functions to the context value
Create a custom hook useCart() that wraps useContext for a cleaner API
Add a shopping cart feature using useState and Context API. Cart state shared across components.
Form validation (manual & with React Hook Form)
React Router v6: BrowserRouter, Routes, Route
Build a Login form with email and password as controlled inputs using useState
Disable the Submit button unless both fields are non-empty
Implement manual validation: check email format with regex; show inline error messages
Reset the form after successful submission
Install react-hook-form; replace manual state with useForm()
Use register() on each input and errors object for validation messages
Add required, minLength, pattern rules declaratively on register()
Use watch() to show a password strength indicator in real-time
Wrap App in <BrowserRouter>; define <Routes> with <Route> for /, /products, /cart, /login
Add a shared <Layout> component with a Navbar that renders on all pages
Use <Link> and <NavLink> in Navbar; observe active class on NavLink
Use useNavigate() to programmatically redirect after form submission
Create a ProtectedRoute component that checks if a JWT token exists in localStorage
If no token, redirect to /login using <Navigate to='/login' replace />
Simulate login/logout by setting/clearing the token and verify route protection
Build Login & Registration forms with validation. Add routing: Home, Products, Cart, Login pages. Guard Cart route.
— Advanced State — Redux Toolkit & API Integration
Why Redux? When to use it vs Context
Integrating with your Spring Boot JWT API
Storing JWT token, Auth state in Redux
Axios interceptors for adding token to requests
Create authSlice with login and logout reducers; define initial state
Configure store with configureStore({ reducer: { auth: authReducer } })
Wrap App in <Provider store={store}> in main.jsx
Create a loginThunk async thunk that POSTs to /api/auth/login
On success, dispatch login(token) action to store the token
Use useSelector(state => state.auth.token) in Navbar to show/hide Login button
Dispatch logout() on a logout button click and clear the stored token
Lab 23.3: RTK Query for Products API
Create a productsApi using createApi with baseQuery pointing to Spring Boot
Define getProducts endpoint (GET /api/v1/products with optional params)
Use auto-generated useGetProductsQuery() hook in ProductList; no useEffect needed
Create an Axios instance with baseURL pointing to Spring Boot
Add a request interceptor to read token from Redux store and attach as Bearer header
Add a response interceptor to catch 401 errors and dispatch logout() automatically
Test that protected API calls succeed with a valid token and redirect to /login when expired
Integrate Redux Toolkit into your app. Manage auth state. RTK Query to fetch products. Auto-attach JWT header.
React component libraries: Material UI or Ant Design
Install Tailwind CSS and configure tailwind.config.js in the Vite project
Replace all inline/CSS styles on ProductCard with Tailwind utility classes
Build a responsive grid: 1 column on mobile, 3 on desktop using Tailwind breakpoints
Add a dark mode toggle using Tailwind dark: variant
Rename App.css to App.module.css; import styles as a module object
Use styles.container, styles.header class names in JSX
Show that CSS Module class names are hashed to prevent collisions
Combine module classes with Tailwind classes using clsx or classnames library
Create a slow-rendering ProductList; use React Profiler in DevTools to measure render time
Wrap ProductCard with React.memo() to prevent re-renders when parent state changes
Use useMemo() to memoize an expensive calculation (e.g., total cart value)
Use useCallback() to stabilize a function reference passed as prop
Replace static imports of AdminPage and CartPage with React.lazy(() => import(...))
Build the app and inspect the dist folder; observe code split chunks
Implement a custom loading skeleton component as the Suspense fallback
Redesign your app UI using Tailwind CSS. Add a loading spinner component. Implement lazy loading for routes.
Full Frontend — Demo: Login with JWT, view products list, add to cart, navigate routes.
Connecting React frontend to Spring Boot backend in production mode
CORS deep-dive: configuration in Spring Boot
End-to-end request flow from React → REST API → DB
Create .env.development and .env.production with different VITE_API_URL values
Access env vars in React using import.meta.env.VITE_API_URL
Build the React app for production (npm run build) and serve with a static server
Configure the production API URL to point to the deployed Spring Boot instance
Observe a CORS preflight error in the browser Network tab when origins mismatch
Fix CORS in Spring Boot using @CrossOrigin or a global WebMvcConfigurer
Understand the difference between simple requests and preflighted requests
Test that OPTIONS preflight returns 200 with correct headers in Postman
Write a test that renders ProductCard and asserts the product name is visible in the DOM
Fire a click event on the Add to Cart button and assert a callback was called
Mock Axios in tests using jest.mock('axios') to avoid real network calls
In Postman, write a test script to assert response status is 200 for GET /products
Save the JWT token from the login response into a Postman environment variable
Use the saved token as a Bearer header in subsequent requests automatically
Run the entire collection with Newman (Postman CLI) from the terminal
Run full stack locally. Write at least 3 integration-style tests. Identify and fix CORS/auth issues.
What is CI/CD? Why it matters
Run Jenkins in Docker: docker run -p 8085:8080 jenkins/jenkins:lts
Complete the setup wizard; install recommended plugins
Create a Freestyle job to pull your Spring Boot project from GitHub and run mvn package
View the build console output and confirm success
Create a Jenkinsfile in the root of your Spring Boot repo with stages: Checkout, Build, Test, Package
Push the Jenkinsfile to GitHub; configure Jenkins Pipeline to read from SCM
Run the pipeline; observe each stage on the Blue Ocean view
Add a post block to always archive the JAR file as a build artifact
Expose Jenkins to the internet using ngrok (for local setup) or use a cloud Jenkins instance
Configure a GitHub webhook: Payload URL = http://your-jenkins/github-webhook/
Push a commit to GitHub and watch Jenkins auto-trigger the pipeline within seconds
Break a unit test intentionally; push and observe the pipeline fail with the failed test report
Add junit('**/target/surefire-reports/*.xml') to the Jenkinsfile to publish test results
View the JUnit test trend graph in Jenkins after 3 builds
Add a post { failure { mail ... } } block to send email on build failure
Configure build status badge in your GitHub README using the Jenkins badge plugin
Create a Jenkins pipeline for your Spring Boot app: pulls from GitHub, builds with Maven, runs JUnit, packages JAR.
What is Docker? VMs vs Containers
Docker commands: build, run, stop, ps, logs, exec
Multi-stage builds for Spring Boot JAR
Install Docker Desktop; run hello-world to verify installation
Run mysql:8 container with docker run -e MYSQL_ROOT_PASSWORD=pass -p 3306:3306 mysql:8
Use docker ps, docker logs, docker exec -it to interact with running containers
Pull the openjdk:21-slim image; inspect its layers with docker history
Write a Dockerfile: FROM openjdk:21-slim, COPY target/app.jar, EXPOSE 8080, ENTRYPOINT
Build the image: docker build -t my-spring-api:1.0 .
Run the container: docker run -p 8080:8080 my-spring-api:1.0
Optimize with a multi-stage build: stage 1 uses Maven to build JAR, stage 2 copies only the JAR
Write a multi-stage Dockerfile: stage 1 (node:18-alpine) runs npm run build, stage 2 (nginx:alpine) serves dist/
Create nginx.conf with try_files $uri /index.html for React Router
Build and run the React container on port 3000
Test the full frontend works inside Docker including route navigation
Create docker-compose.yml with three services: mysql, spring-api, react-app
Use depends_on and healthcheck to ensure MySQL is ready before Spring Boot starts
Use a named volume for MySQL data persistence across restarts
Run docker compose up --build and test the full stack at http://localhost:3000
Write Dockerfiles for both apps. Create docker-compose.yml. Run entire stack with one command.
Cloud deployment options overview (AWS EC2, Elastic Beanstalk, Render, Railway)
Deploying Spring Boot JAR to a cloud service
Setting up environment variables for production
Deploying React app to Netlify or Vercel
Setting up a cloud MySQL database (AWS RDS or PlanetScale)
Lab 28.1: Deploy Spring Boot to Render
Create a free account on Render.com; click New → Web Service
Connect to your GitHub repo; set build command to mvn package -DskipTests and start command to java -jar target/*.jar
Add environment variables: SPRING_DATASOURCE_URL, SPRING_DATASOURCE_USERNAME, SPRING_DATASOURCE_PASSWORD
Wait for the deploy; open the live URL and hit /actuator/health
Create a free MySQL database on Railway or PlanetScale
Copy the connection string and update your Spring Boot environment variables on Render
Run your app and verify it connects to the cloud DB (check Hibernate schema creation logs)
Insert a test record via the API and verify it appears in the cloud DB dashboard
Lab 28.3: Deploy React App to Vercel
Create a free Vercel account; import your React GitHub repository
Set VITE_API_URL environment variable in Vercel to your Render backend URL
Vercel auto-detects Vite; click Deploy and wait ~1 minute for the live URL
Test the full frontend: login, browse products, add to cart – all using the deployed backend
Configure your Spring Boot app to log to stdout (not file) for cloud log capture
View live application logs in the Render dashboard
Access /actuator/health from the live URL to verify the health endpoint is up
Set up a simple uptime monitor on uptimerobot.com to alert you if the service goes down
Deploy your Spring Boot API to Render/Railway. Deploy React app to Vercel/Netlify. Both live on the internet!
System design: define entities, REST API contracts, DB schema
Backend implementation: Spring Boot + Spring Data JPA + MySQL
Git branching strategy for the project
Define your capstone app idea (e.g., online store, task manager, library system)
Draw an ER diagram showing entities and their relationships
Write SQL DDL scripts to create all tables with proper constraints and foreign keys
Commit the ERD image and SQL scripts to a new GitHub repo for the capstone project
Create JPA entity classes mapping to your ER diagram
Define Spring Data JPA repositories with required query methods for each entity
Write service layer classes with business logic; inject repositories
Test service methods with JUnit 5 and Mockito (mock the repository layer)
Create REST controllers for all entities with CRUD endpoints
Secure all /api/** endpoints with JWT; create /auth/login and /auth/register endpoints
Add @Valid validation on all request DTOs
Generate Swagger documentation and verify all endpoints are visible in Swagger UI
Create a main branch for production code and a develop branch for integration
Create a feature/backend-api branch; push and open a PR to develop
Perform a self-code-review: check naming, exception handling, tests, no hardcoded values
Merge the PR; tag the commit v1.0.0-backend
Complete the backend: all entities mapped, REST APIs working, tests passing, pushed to GitHub.
— Capstone Project — Day 2: Frontend, Docker & Live Demo
React frontend: pages, routing, Redux, API integration
Jenkins pipeline: automated build on push
Trainer feedback: code quality, architecture, best practices
Create all required pages in React using React Router v6
Integrate RTK Query to call your deployed Spring Boot API
Implement login/logout flow with Redux Toolkit auth slice
Style all pages with Tailwind CSS; ensure mobile responsiveness
Write Dockerfiles for frontend (React + Nginx) and backend (Spring Boot)
Create docker-compose.yml combining mysql, backend, and frontend services
Test docker compose up --build runs the full stack end-to-end
Push Docker configuration files to the GitHub repo
Create a Jenkinsfile that: checks out code, builds backend JAR, runs JUnit tests, builds Docker image
Trigger the pipeline on every push to the main branch via GitHub webhook
Add a stage to push the Docker image to Docker Hub on successful build
Add a deploy stage that triggers a re-deploy on your cloud hosting platform
Prepare a 10-minute walkthrough: start from the GitHub repo, explain architecture, live demo
Demonstrate: registration, login, core CRUD features, logout — all working on the live URL
Show the GitHub commit history, PR history, and Swagger docs
Present what you would improve next: additional features, performance optimizations, automated E2E tests
GRADUATION: Live demo of your deployed full-stack app. Share your GitHub repo & live URL.
Each participant has: a GitHub profile with a deployed full-stack project, Docker skills, CI/CD pipeline.
